nativeRange.habitat: Exposed seaward reefs, typically found singly or in small groups among mixed algae-coral or rocky reefs at depths of 0–30 m. Tends to be less abundant in lagoons and channels.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Black & White Chromis feeds primarily on zooplankton — small drifting animals suspended in the water column. In captivity it generally accepts frozen meaty foods such as enriched brine shrimp and small mysis shrimp, herbivore preparations, and marine flake foods. Feeding small amounts two or three times daily is generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Izuzuki, 640x480. Tack-sharp, vibrant single adult showing the textbook deep-black anterior, clean white rear half and white tail with electric-blue fin margins; best diagnostic match despite modest resolution.

Bernard DUPONT, 3075x1844. In-situ reef habitat shot showing two black-and-white C. margaritifer (good shoaling context); high-res but fish are moderate-sized in frame and share it with a twospined angelfish.
Rickard Zerpe, 2342x1757. Sharp, well-framed high-res single specimen; body reads brown-purple under warm strobe rather than crisp black, so kept as a detail extra rather than the hero.

summary: The Black & White Chromis (Chromis margaritifer) is a small reef damselfish recognized by its deep black body and contrasting white rear half and tail. Native to exposed Indo-Pacific seaward reefs, it is generally considered one of the hardier chromis and is widely regarded as reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Black & White Chromis is generally considered hardy and relatively peaceful by damselfish standards, though it has been known to display semi-aggressive behavior, particularly toward conspecifics (members of its own species). Keeping a small group of at least three tends to distribute any aggression more evenly and also reflects how the species lives in the wild, where it is found singly or in small groups on exposed reef faces.

Captive specimens may remain somewhat smaller than wild individuals.

The species is also marketed under the older scientific name Pycnochromis margaritifer, which appears in more recent taxonomic literature, though Chromis margaritifer remains the name most widely used in the aquarium trade. It is also sometimes sold as the Bicolor Chromis.

The Black & White Chromis is a diurnal species, meaning it is active during daylight hours and rests at night. On natural reefs it occupies depths from near the surface down to around 30 m.
breedingNotes: The Black & White Chromis is an egg-layer (oviparous), with males observed guarding and aerating demersal eggs — eggs laid directly on a hard surface rather than scattered into the water. Breeding involves distinct pairing behavior. Captive breeding has not been widely reported for this species in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10.2 cm TL), temperature (25–29.3°C), native range, taxonomy, diet, and habitat. Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (22.2–25.6°C vs 25–29.3°C); averaged to 23–28°C. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo Aquatics returned 403; Fishkeeping World returned 404 — FishBase used as the primary cross-verification source.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-laying with distinct pairing behavior; males guard and aerate eggs adhered to a hard substrate. Not commonly bred in home aquaria.
